name: MediaCup
short_description: An ordinary coffee cup invisibly augmented with sensing, processing, and infrared communication so it can broadcast its context into a networked environment.
long_description: MediaCup is an everyday coffee cup whose base houses a temperature sensor, a movement sensor, a microcontroller, and an ultra-low-power infrared transceiver, letting the object sense how it is being handled and report derived states such as drinking, stationary/filled, and hot or cold. It is a foundational ubiquitous-computing experiment in embedding context-awareness into a familiar artifact rather than a screen, and in using the resulting signals (for example the density of co-located cups) as ambient context for surrounding information systems. As a 'smart object' precedent it shows how mundane things can become communicating participants in an environment without changing how people use them.
ai_note: Context states are derived by threshold and rule-based interpretation of temperature and movement sensor signals; no machine learning runs in the system, so the 'some' hint is overridden to 'none'.

images.caption: MediaCup prototype: sensors, microcontroller, and infrared diode built into the base of an ordinary coffee cup.
images.alt_text: A white ceramic coffee cup whose base has been opened to reveal a small circuit board carrying sensors, a microcontroller, and an infrared transmitter.

sources.title: Mediacups: experience with design and use of computer-augmented everyday artefacts
The MediaCup: Awareness Technology Embedded in an Everyday Object
